item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Even if a small wound would close over naturally, a case of abundant regeneration would cause tissue to occasionally scrape against any surface or edge, causing a new wound that would grant a new spurt of regeneration. The additional mass would push the body away, causing new abrasions and wounds that would be cause for new jostling. Imagine trying to move through a forest or damaged building without getting even a tiny scrape.

Sorry if this is necroposting. Kind of new to the site.
I know it's an old thread, but couldn't find this train of thought elsewhere among the comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Shoggoth is a monster, first from Lovecraft and then from Dungeons & Dragons, described as an amorphous blob constantly changing its shape.

Maybe a bit of a necropost, but hope it helps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Darnit I don't get the title and I'm sorta embarrassed I don't cause its probably pretty obvious to others. I know Shoggoth is mentioned in 682, but, wait, does it have something to do with regeneration?

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Since the crosslink was made, I have removed the 'crosslink' tag.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer expired, edits made.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

It seems like SCP-2102 was not the only time GRU-P got its science on with respect to regeneration experiments. In SCP-1760 its implied the creator of the Casket Garden performed chemically induced regeneration experiments for GRU-P in the 50's. In GRU-P casefile "FLY AGARIC", runs similar research as SCP-2102 with fungus. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

I met with the local staff I kept hearing such things about. Comrade doctor Csanyi, their head of research, has some genuinely compelling ideas about test subject suitability index based on basal metabolic rate and a couple other things, though that might have been the result of his barackpalinka.

To this:

I met with the local staff I kept hearing such things about. Comrade doctor Csanyi, their head of research, has some genuinely compelling ideas about test subject suitability index based on basal metabolic rate, the Minsk experiments, and a couple other things, though that might have been the result of his barackpalinka.

Changing this:

Finally! We have found the right composition. It turns out that we needed a little more of that stabilizing agent Jurij (unknown - red.) introduced a few weeks ago. It was staring us in the face all this time. Subject X-16 is responding well, in the medical sense at least; he is as uncooperative as ever. It does not matter. It will be done and over soon, another little step towards our victory. When you think of it, the man is a hero, and he does not even know it.

To this:

Finally! We have found the right composition. It turns out that we needed a little more of that stabilizing agent Jurij (unknown - red.) introduced a few weeks ago. It was staring us in the face all this time. Subject X-16 is responding well, in the medical sense at least; he is as uncooperative as ever. It does not matter. It will be done and over soon, another little step towards our victory. When you think of it, the man is a hero, and he does not even know it.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, for the person who provided the image sequence on the wikipedia page for Abrasion it took 22 days for what looks to be an about 2 cm2, moderately deep scrape on their palm to close completely. Since deep wounds like that heal from the edge in for a normal person, that gives us a baseline skin generation rate of ~ 1mm/day for a given point of surface area for a normal human, or (1.15*10-8)m/s.

That *1015 gives you 115m/s, and over a surface area of 1 cm2 (or .0001m2) gives you 1.15*10-2m3/s. So 86.4 seconds per 1cm2 of damage to generate 1m3 of shoggoth just from that initial wound.

This, however, does not account for the exponentially increasing surface area, because if I read the scip right it continues to "heal" tumor-style over the entire volume. So if I also remember my calculus right, the rate increases at 2.30*10-2m3/s2, meaning it'd only take 4 seconds to generate 1m3, without accounting for secondary factors like internal pressure limiting growth for the inner layers.

Tl;dr: If you see people running away from the direction of SCP-2102's containment chamber, join them and you might live so long as you don't trip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How fast does it generate meat? I see that it does so 1015 faster, but I have no idea how quickly does a baseline human generate meat. If I remove 1 cm² of skin, how quickly will I get 1 m³ of meat? I find it pretty important for the mental picture.

I really am shocked that I hadn't upvoted this before now. It's a terrific concept that integrates GRU-P organically and actually justifies the creator's notes in a way that should be an example to all future authors who try to invoke said trope.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it. This is what would have happened if Wolverine hadn't evolved so smoothly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was on the fence about upvoting or not, but that final entry makes it for me. I'm not sure why exactly, but I think it helps tie together the morality and the humanity of the piece overall in one single note, and gives the thing a cohesiveness emotionally. And I'm a sucker for skips that are emotional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Haha!

Well, I'm from the south of Slovakia, so let's say I was drawing on personal experience for a bit. (Besides, at least if the institute ever appears in further works, it makes for a nice distinction from the stereotypical cold, grey eastern european settings seen in most western fiction.)

Either way, the comment was very much appreciated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Everyone else has already said everything I wanted to: great journal, gripping progression, and a nice creeping horror as you contemplate the poor guy's tortuous existence.

So, I'll just +1 you for the barackpalinka and Dr. Szilard Csanyi, since I married into a Hungarian family and you don't see a lot of Hungarian references here.

Egészségedre!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually didn't care for the diary at the end that much; I kinda had assumed everything in it from what came before and I enjoyed the reclamation logs considerably more. Nonetheless, I still liked the diary enough and the rest of the article so much that I have no doubts about giving a +1.

P.S. - the reason I think I don't much like the diary is the 'note at the end from creator' trope Kalinin mentioned, which I think I have seen played so much that no matter that this is a good example thereof, it still grated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, it was nice and scientific, wasn't it?

<.<

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: VAElynx informed me of that via PM.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! This is very high praise indeed…

I will say that although the general storyline in the diary entries was mine, it was VAELynx who made it come alive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The level of scientific detail throughout this article, starting in the containment procedures and continuing throughout, lend this an aura of authenticity that, when done correctly, can make even mundane skips seem unsettling and uncanny. I would say that at times you veer into alphabet-soup overuse of acronyms, but that's an actual feature of official documentation, so in this case it works quite well.

The skip itself is an example of body horror done well; the description of the process by which this tissue regeneration gone awry occurs is thorough and detailed enough that I can imagine reading it in the executive summary of a medical journal, and it's utterly horrifying to contemplate. Even without the "Merrick" reference in there, this did call to mind the Elephant Man, in the sense of a person being trapped in a prison of their own flesh, which…yeesh.

In the past, I've come down on writers for employing an increasingly common trope; the "explanatory note left at the end by unknown parties that conveniently explains everything". I would submit this article as an example of how to pull off that particular trick without causing eye-rolling. The fact that the notes were recovered in the course of standard intelligence-gathering practices gives their presence context, which is sorely needed for this kind of thing. The story in the notes is allowed to unfold in a natural manner (i.e., they don't start right off the bat with "I am Soviet scientist making super-soldier experiment"), and there's enough detail in there to convince me that I'm reading actual documentation, and not a writer's contrivance.

Finally, I'm not sure if this is because VAE had a hand in the second set of notes, but I really liked that the tone of the dialogue in the recovery mission and the diaries was different and appropriate to its context. It really felt like I was reading a collection of different records as I went through this, which is the reward that comes from attempting dialogue; it's difficult to pull off well, but when you do, it adds a new dimension to a piece that can really give it some staying power with the reader for some time after they've finished it.

Really solid work, Crayne.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another SCP that has to be tortured for the good of mankind, the Foundation, and sadly himself(I'm guessing that these masses may smother him and in the process and could suffocate or smash him under their weight[actually his own weight……..disturbing]). This is amazing +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the high praise. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To me this doesn't violate the "cold, not cruel" axiom, but that line is different for every reader.

Fair enough.

I will say, though, it is extremely well written and for that I will give you an upvote.

+1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, feeding is obviously problematic. Without access to intravenous feeding or a feeding tube, all we can do is administer sustenance via drip feeding. When excising cauterized tissue, we make sure to keep his mouth accessible for that reason, because otherwise, yes, this would all end rather quickly. I could have added that to the containment procedures, but they are already lengthy and it seemed like inane detail in the context of the fictional format.

An artificial coma would be problematic for exactly the same reason as intravenous feeding would be problematic.

And your feelings are totally valid. To me this doesn't violate the "cold, not cruel" axiom, but that line is different for every reader. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
they are invaluable as a means of learning more anout their anomalous properties.

Well, yeah. I get that.

But …

Forty-two years? Blinded, deafened, mute? Restrained?

How is he nourished? A puncture wound (an intravenous puncture wound) would start the tissue reaction. Does he have a naso-gastric tube? If so, add "feeling like you are going to gag or choke all the time" to the torment he is in.

What about elimination? Those burns would not only seal any wound, they would also seal natural bodily openings - so how do they get around that without causing more tissue proliferation?

The only one I can think of that is worse off is poor little 231, and she's only kept alive because of the end-of-the-world problem killing her or otherwise easing her misery would cause.

Maybe if you changed it from being restrained to being kept in an artificial coma. It would make more sense, and be more humane. He's alive to experiment on, but he's "not home" to be tortured.

I'm sorry, this totally a personal feeling here. But it just seems to be sadistic for no very good reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We keep him alive for the same reason we keep all our (as Pig_catapult has referred to them) "blessed with suck" humanoid scips alive: because they are invaluable as a means of learning more anout their anomalous properties. Honestly, you won't be upvoting many humanoid scips if that's your definition of cruel in the context of this setting. ;)

There are scores of scips who are better off if they're allowed to kill themselves, or if we destroy them.

Missed a point you make:

After 42 years, haven't we/they learned all they can from and about him? (And from the Foundation's cold point of view, why waste the resources to keep him alive any more?)

No, we haven't. That's something I tried to imply: even though we've had this guy since 1972, we've never been able to reverse engineer GRU-P's process. Think about it: the Russians were far ahead of everyone, and that was 40 years ago. Imagine what would have happened if they hadn't pushed through and had sat down to work out what was going wrong?

P.S. Is Merrick a reference to the Elephant Man?

And yes, you get Easter Egg brownie points. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh boy. Truly horrific, because of this:

Due to this scarification, SCP-2102 is deaf, mute and blind, and cannot be interviewed.

And he's been in containment since 1972?

I really love the writing, I love the story and I would gladly give you an upvote except for one small itchy point:

The Foundation is cold, not cruel. Why are they keeping this poor, poor man alive? After 42 years, haven't we/they learned all they can from and about him? (And from the Foundation's cold point of view, why waste the resources to keep him alive any more?)

Settle that issue somehow, and I will upvote in seconds.

P.S. Is Merrick a reference to the Elephant Man?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's quite creepy, I must say, and very entertaining. The thought of infinite growing tissue really made my skin crawl, so take my upvote! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks!

I've edited the article for your suggestion. Google agrees with you 100%. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unsettling and well-paced. The journal does a nice job of telling the science story in an accessible way. +1

I think the term contra-revolutionary is a little out of place unless you are translating from Spanish; in Soviet context counter-revolutionary is far more common.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why is he currently 5'6" and 105 pounds? He was obviously much larger during that initial containment log; did the napalm burn off his extra tissue while leaving the "main" body unharmed? Once I figured out what this guy was doing (which wasn't easy, I'll have you know), I kind of expected him to be a little bigger than normal-guy-size.

He got Tetsuo-sized during that particular UER/2102 event, yes. The cauterization provided by the incendiary bombs gave us time to excise the excess tissue and bring in the guys with flamethrowers. Eventually we were able to reduce him to a manageable size. His current form is roughly humanoid, but he's really just a mass of scars containing his vital organs.

And what's with all the "red" tags on the identifications in the diary? EDIT: I have been informed they stand for "redactor", as "ed." would be used for editors' notes.

Well, don't I get away scott free there? :P

Actually, "red." is the Dutch variant of "ed." and I simply didn't realize I was using it. It's a good thing it also stands for "redactor" then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They pulled Wolverine out of the gulag and turned him into a monster. I really like this. Thoughts and questions follow.

Why is he currently 5'6" and 105 pounds? He was obviously much larger during that initial containment log; did the napalm burn off his extra tissue while leaving the "main" body unharmed? Once I figured out what this guy was doing (which wasn't easy, I'll have you know), I kind of expected him to be a little bigger than normal-guy-size.

And what's with all the "red" tags on the identifications in the diary? EDIT: I have been informed they stand for "redactor", as "ed." would be used for editors' notes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There ae distinct phases to the wound healing process; the scip never enters the latter ones where the wound closes and knits together. It just keeps producing new tissue. Cauterization works because it artificially closs the wound.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't actually quite get how this works. It grows flesh until all the wounds are healed, I think, but then why would it grow to the massive size seen in the recovery? Shouldn't it just grow over the wound and then stop? Or does it somehow grow around the wound, without healing or sealing it? That would be weird. Or does it only need wounds to start growing, and then just grows forever? Then I don't get while cauterization works. Wouldn't it just continue growing even after being cauterized?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm really not exactly sure what it is, but I like it! +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
X-16 was anomalous before he ended up in GRU-P custody, but we're unsure if his anomalous condition was present before his time in the uranium mines, or a result of his time there.

The experiment worked because the science was solid, but it needed a little jump-start to get results. X-16 was that jump-start.

Thanks, by the way. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I usually read things pretty quickly, but I had to slow down a bit in order to understand a lot of the technical stuff at the beginning. Of course, that's probably just a personal preference sort of thing.

One question, though: was X-16 already anomalous before the experiments? He spent 15 years in a uranium mine with no permanent damage, he heals twice as fast as a normal humans, did the experiment only "work" because of this "healing factor", or did I interpret it completely wrong?

Overall, really great. "Mound of flesh" is kind of overused, but you made it super interesting.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Got Shoggoth?"

Oh you!
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… heeeelloooo, do I know you, Got Shoggoth? +1

(Love the diary. :D)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to Riemann, Dr. Reach, Fantem, FortuneFavorsBold and ChrisAKAPiefish for helping with the draft.

Extra special thanks to VAELynx for his work on the diary, and to EchoFourDelta for supplying me with the killswitch (though originally for use against SCP-366).
